---
title: "2.2.1 The need to check for errors"
subtitle: "Data Transmission P54"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)

## Why Error Detection Matters

### Transmission errors are inevitable

When data is transmitted, there is always a risk that it may be <span class="mark">corrupted, lost or even gained</span>. Errors can occur during data transmission due to:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Interference</span> — all types of cable can suffer from electrical interference, which can cause data to be corrupted or even lost
- <span class="mark">Problems during packet switching</span> — this can lead to data loss or it is even possible to <span class="mark">gain data</span>!
- <span class="mark">Skewing of data</span> — this occurs during parallel data transmission and can cause data corruption if the bits arrive out of synchronisation
:::

## Why Error Checking Matters

### Computers can't understand corrupted text

Checking for errors is important since computers are unable to understand text if the words are not recognised by its built-in dictionary. Look at the following example of corrupted text:

> "I cnduo't bvleiee taht I culod aulaclty uesdtannrd waht I was rdnaieg. Unisg the icndeblire pweor of the hmuan mnid, aocdcrnig to rseecrah at Cmabridge Uinervtisy, it dseno't mttaer in waht oderr the lterets in a wrod are..."

::: {.tight-list}
- A <span class="mark">human</span> would probably have little problem understanding this text
- But a <span class="mark">computer would be unable to make any sense</span> of it
- Data corruption is therefore a <span class="mark">very real problem</span> to a computer
- This is why error checking is such an <span class="mark">important part of computer technology</span>
:::

## Error Detection Methods

### Three ways to check data after transmission

There are a number of ways data can be checked for errors following transmission:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Parity checks</span> — based on counting the number of 1-bits in a byte
- <span class="mark">Checksum</span> — a calculated value sent after a block of data
- <span class="mark">Echo check</span> — receiver sends data back to sender for comparison
:::

Each method has different error detection capability and overhead.

## Classroom Check

### Understand why error checking is necessary

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">errors occur due to interference, packet switching problems, and data skewing</span>
- data can be <span class="mark">corrupted, lost or even gained</span> during transmission
- <span class="mark">computers cannot understand corrupted text</span> (unlike humans) — error checking is essential
- three methods covered: <span class="mark">parity checks, checksum, echo check</span>
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Parity, Checksum, Echo Check

The next deck covers specific error detection methods and how they work.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)
